calibration_gate.plain_language_result: Probability estimates improved, but ranking quality worsened far beyond the allowed limit. SpecSafe therefore blocked automated use.

cases.plain_language_result: All three policies performed the same under flat capacity.
All three policies performed the same under light load.
The adaptive policy was too conservative under moderate load.
The adaptive policy avoided large losses under saturated load.
The adaptive policy avoided losses under jagged capacity.
The adaptive policy matched fixed length and beat the threshold baseline.

final_interpretation: Adaptive scheduling can be useful, but it is not universally better. Its value depends on trustworthy confidence signals and hard activation gates.
non_claims: No global policy winner is established.
No scheduler or confidence candidate is promoted for runtime control.
No production throughput, latency, cost, or serving result is established.

policies.plain_language_description: Always verifies the same amount of work.
Uses one confidence cutoff in every condition.
Changes verification effort using calibrated confidence and available capacity.

quick_summary: Adaptive verification helped under some conditions, was neutral under others, and lost in one. When the real confidence signal failed its safety gate, SpecSafe blocked activation.

short_description: AI reliability case study on adaptive verification.
supported_claims: The controlled synthetic corpus contains adaptive wins, neutral cases, and one loss.
Unsafe retrospective controls failed causal safety and were excluded.
Probability calibration improved on the independent holdout.
Ranking safety regressed beyond tolerance, so activation was blocked.
The public Dataset preserves the exact governed negative-evidence release.
tested_question: Can an AI verification policy spend limited compute more intelligently than fixed rules without using forbidden future information?
title: SpecSafe — When Should AI Spend More Compute?
